findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py exports two conflicting error-summary writers with different schemas for the same output filenames
evidence: reporting/__init__.py:32,65: both save_error_summary (from pipeline_reporter) and ErrorAggregator (from error_aggregator) are exported from the same package — both write error_summary.json and error_summary.md with incompatible schemas

pipeline_reporter.py:460-493: save_error_summary writes {total_errors, errors_by_type, errors}; error_aggregator.py:198-222: ErrorAggregator.save_report writes {actionable_fixes, total_warnings, warnings_by_type} — same filename, different schema
Callers importing from infrastructure.reporting get two symbols that produce conflicting artefacts with no documented canonical choice
findings.suggestion: Remove save_error_summary and generate_error_markdown from pipeline_reporter.py. Route all callers through ErrorAggregator.save_report() as the single canonical writer. If callers only have a raw list[dict], add ErrorAggregator.from_dicts() to hydrate before saving.

findings.summary: Large swath of infrastructure modules use deprecated `from typing import Dict, List, Tuple` instead of the built-in generics used consistently in core/
evidence: audit_orchestrator.py:12: `from typing import Any, Dict, List` then uses `Dict[str, set[str]]` at line 149 and `Dict[str, List[Any]]` at line 154
output_validator.py:10: `from typing import Any, Dict` then uses `Dict[str, Any]` throughout

core/ files (errors.py, health_check.py, logging_helpers.py) consistently use built-in `dict[str, Any]` and `list[str]` without importing from typing — the modern style is established in core but not followed in validation/ and rendering/
logging_formatters.py:8: `from typing import ClassVar, Dict` — Dict is unnecessary since Python 3.9; ClassVar is still needed
findings.suggestion: Replace `Dict[X, Y]` / `List[X]` / `Tuple[X, ...]` with lowercase built-in generics `dict[X, Y]` / `list[X]` / `tuple[X, ...]` in all non-core modules. The `from __future__ import annotations` already present in every file makes this safe. Remove the now-unused `Dict`, `List`, `Tuple` names from the typing imports.

findings.summary: `format_duration` is defined in `logging_helpers.py` but also re-exported through `logging_utils.py` and `core/__init__.py`, creating an ambiguous canonical location
evidence: logging_helpers.py:35: `def format_duration(seconds: float) -> str:` — definition lives here
logging_utils.py:25: `from infrastructure.core.logging_helpers import format_duration, format_error_with_suggestions` — re-exported
logging_utils.py:399: `'format_duration'` in `__all__`
core/__init__.py:27-32: `from .logging_utils import format_duration, get_logger, ...` — re-exported a second time
pipeline_reporter.py:16: `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import format_duration, get_logger` — callers import from logging_utils, not the canonical helper module
Result: the function has three import paths with no documented canonical one
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical import path (`infrastructure.core.logging_helpers` or `infrastructure.core.logging_utils`) and remove the re-export from the other. Update callers to use the canonical path. The `core/__init__.py` re-export is fine as a convenience facade but the intermediate re-export in `logging_utils` only adds indirection.

findings.summary: `pipeline_reporter.save_error_summary()` and `ErrorAggregator.save_report()` both write `error_summary.json` + `error_summary.md` to an output directory with different schemas, creating conflicting write paths for the same artefact
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py:460-493: `save_error_summary(errors: list[dict], output_dir)` writes `error_summary.json` with keys `{total_errors, errors_by_type, errors}` and delegates markdown to `generate_error_markdown()`

error_aggregator.py:198-222: `ErrorAggregator.save_report(output_dir)` writes the same filenames `error_summary.json` and `error_summary.md` with a richer schema adding `{actionable_fixes, total_warnings, warnings_by_type}`

reporting/__init__.py:32,65: both `save_error_summary` (from pipeline_reporter) and `ErrorAggregator` (from error_aggregator) are exported from the same package surface, with no guidance on which to use
The two functions accept different input types (raw `list[dict]` vs `ErrorAggregator` instance) and produce incompatible JSON schemas for the same filename
findings.suggestion: Remove the standalone `save_error_summary` / `generate_error_markdown` functions from `pipeline_reporter.py`. All callers should use `ErrorAggregator.save_report()` as the single writer. If callers only have a raw `list[dict]`, add a `ErrorAggregator.from_dicts(errors)` class method to hydrate the aggregator before saving.

findings.summary: `validate_copied_outputs` and `validate_output_structure` both reverse-engineer the project name and repo root by parsing `output_dir.parts` instead of accepting them as explicit parameters, creating fragile implicit coupling to the directory layout
evidence: output_validator.py:39-48: `path_parts = output_dir.parts; if 'output' in path_parts: output_idx = path_parts.index('output'); project_name = output_dir.name` — project identity derived by scanning the path string
output_validator.py:83-93: same pattern repeated to reconstruct `repo_root` from `path_parts[:output_idx]` when the PDF is not in the output directory
output_validator.py:421: `validate_output_structure` repeats the same `output_dir.parent.name == 'output'` heuristic to infer project name
The approach silently produces `project_name = None` for any path that does not contain the literal string 'output', making behaviour unpredictable for projects with unusual paths
findings.suggestion: Add `project_name: str | None = None` and `repo_root: Path | None = None` as explicit parameters to `validate_copied_outputs` and `validate_output_structure`. Callers already know the project name and repo root at the point of invocation; the path-parsing logic can then be deleted entirely.

findings.summary: requests is used in core infrastructure but declared only as an optional LLM group dependency
evidence: infrastructure/llm/utils/ollama.py:26: `import requests` (top-level, not guarded) — this module is reachable without the llm optional group
infrastructure/publishing/api.py:9: `import requests` (top-level) — publishing is not behind an optional group

pyproject.toml:57-59: requests is declared only under [dependency-groups] llm, not in [project] dependencies — callers of publishing.api or llm.utils.ollama will fail at import if only core deps are installed
findings.suggestion: Either move requests into [project] dependencies alongside numpy/matplotlib/pypdf/pyyaml, or guard the import in each module with try/except ImportError and raise a clear error at call time (following the _optional_deps.py pattern already established for psutil).

findings.summary: SystemHealthChecker._check_dependencies hardcodes a dep list that diverges from pyproject.toml
evidence: health_check.py:176-182: critical_deps = ['numpy', 'matplotlib', 'requests', 'pyyaml', 'psutil'] — includes requests and psutil as 'critical' but both are optional in pyproject.toml; pypdf (a declared core dep) is absent from the list
This creates a false 'missing' signal for psutil/requests on installs without optional groups, and misses pypdf failures entirely
findings.suggestion: Drive the health check dep list from pyproject.toml at runtime (e.g., read [project].dependencies via importlib.metadata) or at minimum align the hardcoded list with actual [project] dependencies and demote requests/psutil to the existing optional_deps check block.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/suite_runner.py has no dedicated test file despite being a critical execution path
evidence: tests/infra_tests/reporting/ contains 12 test files covering pipeline_reporter, coverage_parser, executive_reporter, html_templates, etc., but no test_suite_runner.py file exists
suite_runner.py is the path that actually invokes pytest as a subprocess and streams output — errors here silently swallow test failures rather than propagating them
findings.suggestion: Add tests/infra_tests/reporting/test_suite_runner.py covering at minimum: successful pytest invocation on a trivial temp test file, failed invocation returning non-zero exit code, and coverage file path resolution. Use real temp pytest runs (no mocks) per project policy.

findings.summary: _run_infrastructure_tests is a one-line private wrapper that only calls run_infrastructure_tests with no added policy
evidence: pipeline.py:396-398: `def _run_infrastructure_tests(self) -> bool: / """Run infrastructure tests.""" / return self.run_infrastructure_tests()` — the private method adds no logic and exists solely to satisfy a stage-dispatch table that could reference the public method directly
This is the exact pass-through wrapper anti-pattern: private wrapper with docstring restating its one-line body
findings.suggestion: Replace the `StageSpec("Infrastructure Tests", self._run_infrastructure_tests)` entry in pipeline.py with `StageSpec("Infrastructure Tests", self.run_infrastructure_tests)` and delete the `_run_infrastructure_tests` method.

findings.summary: Multi-line Args/Returns docstrings on trivial one-to-three-line methods throughout logging_progress.py and logging_formatters.py
evidence: logging_progress.py:123-130: Spinner.__init__ has a 4-line Args docstring for message/stream/delay — parameters are self-documenting from their names and types
logging_progress.py:252-257: StreamingProgress.update has Args block for `increment` and `custom_message` on a 3-line body
logging_formatters.py:19-27: JSONFormatter.format has Args/Returns docblock for a standard logging.Formatter override where the signature is defined by the base class
logging_formatters.py:60-68: TemplateFormatter.format repeats the same Args/Returns pattern for a method with identical shape
findings.suggestion: Collapse multi-line Args/Returns docstrings for methods whose parameter names and type annotations already convey the information. A single-line summary is sufficient for trivial methods. Reserve Args/Returns blocks for non-obvious parameters or non-obvious return shapes.

findings.summary: The logging subsystem is fragmented across five sibling files in infrastructure/core/ with ambiguous boundaries
evidence: logging_utils.py re-exports format_duration and format_error_with_suggestions from logging_helpers.py via its __all__, making logging_utils.py a hub that consumers import from even when the implementation lives elsewhere — confirmed by pipeline_reporter.py:16 importing format_duration from logging_utils
logging_progress.py imports from logging_helpers.py (format_duration) and logging_constants.py — three-hop import chain for a single utility function

Five files (constants, formatters, helpers, progress, utils) all named logging_* in the same flat directory; a single infrastructure/core/logging/ subpackage would make the boundary explicit and navigable
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the five logging_* modules into an infrastructure/core/logging/ subpackage with an __init__.py that re-exports the public surface (get_logger, setup_logger, log_operation, etc.). This moves the fragmented logging concern under one directory boundary without changing any external import paths if __init__.py re-exports are maintained.

findings.summary: The reporting/ package conflates three distinct responsibilities — HTML generation, test-result persistence, and pipeline orchestration — within a single flat package with no sub-grouping by concern
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py lines 226-384: embeds a full HTML renderer (generate_html_report) with inline CSS alongside pipeline data assembly, blurring the line between data and presentation
suite_summary_generator.py lines 358-411: run_test_summary_generation() writes to disk and prints console output — an orchestration concern — inside a module whose declared role is report generation

dashboard_generator.py: separately generates matplotlib charts and HTML templates, yet lives at the same flat level as text report generators, giving the package two incompatible rendering paradigms with no grouping
findings.suggestion: Split infrastructure/reporting/ into sub-packages: data/ (parsers, aggregators), renderers/ (HTML, markdown, charts), and runner/ (orchestration entry points). This makes ownership boundaries predictable and allows each concern to evolve independently.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor._run_infrastructure_tests is a single-line private wrapper that adds no policy, only calling run_infrastructure_tests()
evidence: pipeline.py line 396-398: _run_infrastructure_tests delegates unconditionally to run_infrastructure_tests(), which on line 392-394 delegates unconditionally to _run_script(). Three hops, zero added policy at any level.

The public run_infrastructure_tests() exists solely for the multi-project orchestrator, but the private _run_infrastructure_tests() is then only used internally by _build_stage_list, making one of the two names redundant.
findings.suggestion: Remove _run_infrastructure_tests and register run_infrastructure_tests directly in _build_stage_list. If the public name must exist for external callers, make it the only entry point and reference it directly in _build_stage_list — eliminating the indirection layer.

findings.summary: In suite_runner.run_test_suite, the branch `if exit_code == 0: pass` is dead control flow that adds noise without behavior
evidence: suite_runner.py lines 286-292: `if exit_code == 0: pass` — the pass branch does nothing and exists only to anchor the elif/else chain. The comment '# Caller logs success' documents the absence of action, not an intent that requires a branch.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `if exit_code == 0: pass` arm entirely and invert the logic: `if exit_code != 0:` containing the error/warning branches. This reduces nesting and makes the control flow direct.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py mixes high-abstraction report assembly with low-abstraction inline HTML string building and duplicates section-rendering logic across markdown and HTML renderers
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py lines 125-223 and 226-384: both _generate_pipeline_markdown and generate_html_report independently iterate over the same optional sections (test_results, validation_results, performance_metrics, error_summary, output_statistics) with near-identical guard logic (if report.X:) and field lookups. Adding a new section requires editing both functions.

generate_html_report (line 226) builds multi-hundred-line HTML strings with f-string concatenation inline with the section-rendering logic, mixing template concerns with data-access concerns in a single function body.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared section descriptor list (name, attribute, render_fn) iterated by both renderers, so adding a new pipeline report section requires one addition instead of two. Alternatively, move HTML generation to infrastructure/reporting/html_templates.py (which already exists) so pipeline_reporter.py contains only data assembly.
